claim: The Transformer achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task.
claim_context: The Transformer achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, outperforming all prior models.
notes: Table 2, row 'Transformer (big)' — exact number stated.

claim: The base Transformer model uses 8 attention heads in each multi-head attention layer.
claim_context: The base model uses 8 attention heads in each multi-head attention layer.
notes: Table 1 model dimensions — h=8 for base model.

claim: The Transformer uses recurrent layers to model sequential dependencies between tokens.
claim_context: The Transformer uses recurrent layers to model sequential dependencies.
notes: The paper explicitly replaces recurrence entirely with self-attention.

claim: The Transformer requires pre-training on a large unlabeled corpus before fine-tuning on downstream tasks.
claim_context: The Transformer requires pre-training on a large unlabeled corpus before fine-tuning on downstream tasks.
notes: Pre-training is BERT, not the original Transformer. Vaswani trains end-to-end.

claim: BERT is pre-trained using a masked language modeling objective on large unlabeled text corpora.
claim_context: BERT is pre-trained using a masked language modeling objective on large unlabeled text corpora.
notes: Core contribution of the paper — stated explicitly in abstract and Section 3.1.

claim: BERT achieves state-of-the-art results on eleven natural language processing tasks.
claim_context: BERT achieves state-of-the-art on eleven natural language processing tasks including GLUE and SQuAD.
notes: Abstract: 'new state-of-the-art results on eleven NLP tasks'.

claim: BERT requires no pre-training and is trained end-to-end on each individual downstream task.
claim_context: BERT requires no pre-training and is trained end-to-end on each downstream task.
notes: Opposite of truth — pre-training is the entire premise of BERT.

claim: BERT achieves perfect accuracy on the MNLI benchmark.
claim_context: BERT achieves perfect accuracy on the MNLI benchmark.
notes: BERT achieves 86.7% on MNLI-m, not perfect accuracy.

claim: GPT-3 contains 175 billion parameters.
claim_context: GPT-3 contains 175 billion parameters, making it the largest language model at time of publication.
notes: Table 2.1 — 175B parameters stated explicitly.

claim: GPT-3 achieves 76.2% accuracy on TriviaQA in the few-shot setting without any gradient updates.
claim_context: GPT-3 achieves 76.2% accuracy on TriviaQA in the few-shot setting without any gradient updates.
notes: Table 3.5 — 76.2% on TriviaQA closed-book few-shot.

claim: GPT-3 requires fine-tuning on each task to achieve competitive performance on NLP benchmarks.
claim_context: GPT-3 requires fine-tuning on each task to achieve competitive performance on NLP benchmarks.
notes: The central thesis is few-shot learning without fine-tuning. Fine-tuning is explicitly not needed.

claim: GPT-3 outperforms all fine-tuned models on every SuperGLUE benchmark task.
claim_context: GPT-3 outperforms all fine-tuned models on every SuperGLUE benchmark task.
notes: GPT-3 does not beat fine-tuned SOTA on SuperGLUE overall — Table 3.8 shows it below fine-tuned models.

claim: Residual networks won the ILSVRC 2015 classification competition with a top-5 error of 3.57%.
claim_context: Residual networks won the ILSVRC 2015 classification competition with a top-5 error of 3.57%.
notes: Abstract states 3.57% top-5 error, first place on ILSVRC 2015.

claim: Residual networks allow training of networks with more than 100 layers without performance degradation.
claim_context: ResNets allow training of networks with more than 100 layers without degradation.
notes: Core claim of the paper — 152-layer ResNet outperforms shallower models.

claim: Plain networks show lower training error than residual networks as depth increases beyond 20 layers.
claim_context: Plain networks show lower training error than residual networks as depth increases beyond 20 layers.
notes: Figure 1 shows the opposite: plain nets have higher training error as depth grows — this is the degradation problem ResNets solve.

claim: ResNets achieve zero training error on CIFAR-10 with any depth above 1000 layers.
claim_context: ResNets achieve zero training error on CIFAR-10 with any depth above 1000 layers.
notes: No such claim exists in the paper. They test up to 1202 layers with specific results, not zero error.

claim: ViT trained on JFT-300M achieves 88.55% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet.
claim_context: Vision Transformer achieves 88.55% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet when pre-trained on JFT-300M.
notes: Table 2 — ViT-H/14 achieves 88.55% top-1 on ImageNet.

claim: ViT outperforms ResNets when trained on datasets smaller than ImageNet such as CIFAR-10.
claim_context: ViT outperforms ResNets when trained on small datasets like CIFAR-10.
notes: The paper explicitly states ViT underperforms CNNs when not pre-trained on large datasets (Section 3.1).

claim: ViT requires large-scale pre-training to match or exceed the performance of convolutional networks.
claim_context: ViT requires significantly large datasets to achieve competitive accuracy compared to convolutional networks.
notes: Explicitly stated: 'ViT attains excellent results when pre-trained at sufficient scale'.

claim: GANs consist of a generative model and a discriminative model trained simultaneously in an adversarial game.
claim_context: Generative adversarial networks consist of a generative model and a discriminative model trained simultaneously.
notes: Core definition stated in abstract and Section 1.

claim: The original GAN paper demonstrates high-quality image generation on ImageNet with realistic human-level samples.
claim_context: The original GAN paper demonstrates high-quality image generation on ImageNet.
notes: Original GAN paper experiments on MNIST, TFD, and CIFAR-10 — not ImageNet.

claim: GANs are proven to converge to a unique global optimum regardless of network architecture.
claim_context: GANs are proven to always converge to a unique global optimum regardless of network architecture.
notes: Paper proves convergence only for the minimax game under ideal conditions (infinite capacity). Practical convergence is explicitly acknowledged as an open problem.

claim: Word vector representations can capture semantic relationships through vector arithmetic such as king minus man plus woman approximately equals queen.
claim_context: Word vectors can capture semantic relationships through vector arithmetic such as king minus man plus woman equals queen.
notes: Section 4, Table 2 — classic analogy example demonstrated.

claim: Training Word2Vec embeddings requires millions of GPU hours even on small corpora.
claim_context: Training Word2Vec requires millions of GPU hours even on small corpora.
notes: The paper highlights efficiency — e.g. 100B words trained in less than a day on a single machine.

claim: Adam computes adaptive learning rates for each parameter using estimates of first and second moments of the gradients.
claim_context: Adam computes adaptive learning rates using estimates of first and second moments of the gradients.
notes: Algorithm 1 and Section 2 — exact description of Adam.

claim: Adam converges faster than SGD on all convex optimization problems including non-smooth objectives.
claim_context: Adam is proven to converge faster than SGD on all convex optimization problems.
notes: Theorem 1 proves regret bound for online convex optimization but does not claim universally faster than SGD across all problems.

claim: Dropout prevents overfitting by randomly setting unit activations to zero during training with probability p.
claim_context: Dropout prevents overfitting by randomly setting unit activations to zero during training with probability p.
notes: Abstract and Section 2 — core definition of dropout.

claim: Dropout consistently slows down training convergence without any improvement in test accuracy.
claim_context: Dropout consistently slows down training convergence without any improvement in test accuracy.
notes: Paper shows dropout does slow training slightly but dramatically improves test accuracy — that is the entire point.

claim: This fictional method achieves perfect accuracy on all benchmarks simultaneously.
claim_context: This fictional method achieves perfect accuracy on all benchmarks simultaneously.
notes: Paper does not exist — should always return unverifiable.
